title: Lesson 2: Doubling and Halving
subtopics: Balancing Trick — One Doubles, One Halves
learningGoals: Double and halve any number instantly without paper.
Use the balancing trick to make hard multiplications easy.
Apply ×5, ×25, ÷5, ÷25 shortcuts using doubling and halving.
coreIdeas: Doubling and halving are the fastest mental operations — build every shortcut on them.
The balancing trick: if one number doubles and the other halves, the product stays the same.
starterPractice: Warm-up: double 24 and double 37 in your head.
Independent: use the ×25 trick to solve 25 × 36.

teachingScript.teacherLine: Scene 1: Doubling. To multiply by 2, just add the number to itself. Split tens and units for speed.
teachingScript.boardAction: Show 2×37: split 30+7, double each: 60+14=74. Show the split method clearly.
teachingScript.checkpointPrompt: What is 2 × 46? Use the split method.
teachingScript.microPractice: Micro-practice: double 53 and double 78 in your head.

teachingScript.teacherLine: Scene 2: ×4 means double twice. First double, then double again.
teachingScript.boardAction: Show 4×23: 23→46→92. Draw two doubling arrows. Show 4×54: 54→108→216.
teachingScript.checkpointPrompt: Solve 4 × 23 by doubling twice. What do you get after the first double?
teachingScript.microPractice: Micro-practice: solve 4×15 and 4×32 by doubling twice.

teachingScript.teacherLine: Scene 3: ×8 means double three times. Three arrows, three doublings.
teachingScript.checkpointPrompt: Solve 8 × 11. What is the number after the second doubling?
teachingScript.microPractice: Micro-practice: solve 8×13 by doubling three times.

teachingScript.teacherLine: Scene 4: Halving is the reverse of doubling. Split odd numbers as whole + remainder.
teachingScript.checkpointPrompt: Halve 138 using the split method. What is 100÷2?

teachingScript.teacherLine: Scene 5: ÷4 means halve twice. Two arrows going the other way.
teachingScript.checkpointPrompt: Divide 48 by 4. After the first halving, what do you have?
teachingScript.microPractice: Micro-practice: solve 64÷4 and 100÷4 by halving twice.

teachingScript.subtopic: Balancing Trick — One Doubles, One Halves
teachingScript.teacherLine: Scene 6: The Balancing Trick. If one number doubles and the other halves, the product stays the same. This is the most powerful shortcut.
teachingScript.boardAction: Show 16×5: halve 16→8, double 5→10, so 8×10=80. Show scale balance visual.
teachingScript.checkpointPrompt: For 16×5, which number did we halve and which did we double?

teachingScript.teacherLine: Scene 7: ×5 shortcut. Multiply by 10 first (add a zero), then halve. Super fast!
teachingScript.checkpointPrompt: Solve 5×14. What do you get after multiplying by 10?
teachingScript.microPractice: Micro-practice: solve 5×28 and 5×56 using the ×5 trick.

teachingScript.teacherLine: Scene 8: ×25 shortcut. Multiply by 100 first, then divide by 4 (halve twice).
teachingScript.checkpointPrompt: For 25×12, after multiplying by 100 you get 1200. Now halve it twice.
teachingScript.microPractice: Micro-practice: solve 25×8 and 25×20 using the ×100 ÷4 trick.

teachingScript.teacherLine: Scene 9: ÷5 and ÷25 reversal. ÷5 = double then divide by 10. ÷25 = ×4 then divide by 100.
teachingScript.checkpointPrompt: Divide 70 by 5. After doubling 70, what do you get?
teachingScript.microPractice: Micro-practice: solve 90÷5 and 300÷25 using the reverse trick.

screenplay.teacherLine: Welcome to Lesson 2 — Doubling and Halving! These are the fastest mental operations in Vedic Math. Every shortcut we learn today is built on just two moves: double and halve. Let us start with the most basic — multiplying by 2.
screenplay.boardAction: Show lesson title and overview of the doubling chain: ×2 → ×4 → ×8
screenplay.checkpointPrompt: What does doubling a number mean?

svgAnimation.text: The fastest mental shortcuts in Vedic Math

screenplay.teacherLine: To double any number, split it into tens and units. Double each part separately, then add. Watch: 2 times 37. Thirty doubled is sixty. Seven doubled is fourteen. Sixty plus fourteen equals seventy-four.
Your turn now. Double 46 using the split method. Split it into tens and units first.
Now let us extend the doubling chain. Four equals two times two. So to multiply by 4, just double twice. Two steps, two arrows.
screenplay.checkpointPrompt: How many times do we double to multiply by 4?

screenplay.teacherLine: Let us try four times twenty-three. First double: twenty-three becomes forty-six. Second double: forty-six becomes ninety-two. Done — no long multiplication needed.
Your turn. Solve four times fifty-four by doubling twice. What is fifty-four doubled?
Extending further — eight equals two times two times two. So multiply by 8 with three doublings. Three arrows.
screenplay.boardAction: Show chain: n → 2n → 4n → 8n with three ×2 arrows
screenplay.checkpointPrompt: How many times do we double to multiply by 8?

screenplay.teacherLine: Watch: eight times eleven. Eleven doubled is twenty-two. Twenty-two doubled is forty-four. Forty-four doubled is eighty-eight. And eight times twenty-five: twenty-five to fifty to one hundred to two hundred.
Your turn. Eight times fourteen. Start doubling. What is fourteen doubled?
Now the reverse. Halving is dividing by two. For odd tens, split the number: halve the hundreds and tens part, halve the units, combine.
screenplay.checkpointPrompt: What is the first step to halve 138?
screenplay.fallbackHint: Split into 100 and 38, then halve each.

screenplay.teacherLine: For even numbers it is simple: forty-eight halved is twenty-four. For bigger numbers, split first. One hundred and thirty-eight: one hundred halved is fifty, thirty-eight halved is nineteen. Add them: sixty-nine.
screenplay.boardAction: Show 48÷2=24, then 138÷2 with full split working

screenplay.teacherLine: Your turn. Halve three hundred and seventy-six. Split it first.
Dividing by four: halve twice. Same chain as multiplying by four, but running backwards.
screenplay.boardAction: Show reverse chain: 4n → 2n → n with ÷2 arrows
screenplay.checkpointPrompt: To divide by 4, how many times do we halve?

screenplay.teacherLine: Forty-eight divided by four: forty-eight halved is twenty-four, twenty-four halved is twelve. Ninety-two divided by four: ninety-two halved is forty-six, forty-six halved is twenty-three.
Your turn. Divide two hundred and sixteen by four. Halve it once first.

screenplay.subtopic: Balancing Trick — One Doubles, One Halves
screenplay.teacherLine: This is the most powerful trick in this lesson. If you double one number and halve the other, the product stays exactly the same. Like a see-saw — one side goes up, the other comes down, but they stay balanced.
screenplay.boardAction: Draw balance scale: 16×5 on one side = 8×10 on the other = 80
screenplay.checkpointPrompt: If we halve 16 to get 8, what must we do to 5 to keep the product the same?

screenplay.subtopic: Balancing Trick — One Doubles, One Halves
screenplay.teacherLine: Sixteen times five looks hard. But halve sixteen to get eight, double five to get ten. Now eight times ten is eighty — instant! Let us try fourteen times eight: halve fourteen to seven, double eight to sixteen. Seven times sixteen is one-twelve.
screenplay.checkpointPrompt: What is 14 × 8 using the balancing trick?

screenplay.subtopic: Balancing Trick — One Doubles, One Halves
screenplay.teacherLine: Your turn. Twenty-five times twelve. Can you use the balancing trick to make one of these a round number?
screenplay.boardAction: Show 25×12 — hint: double 25 to 50, halve 12 to 6
screenplay.checkpointPrompt: What is 25 × 12 using the balancing trick?

screenplay.teacherLine: Multiplying by five is super easy when you know this trick. Five is half of ten. So multiply by ten first, then halve.
screenplay.checkpointPrompt: Why is ×5 the same as ×10 then ÷2?

screenplay.teacherLine: Five times thirty-six: multiply thirty-six by ten — three hundred and sixty — then halve — one hundred and eighty. Five times one hundred and forty-two: one thousand four hundred and twenty, halved is seven hundred and ten.
Your turn. Five times fourteen. Multiply by ten first.
Twenty-five is one quarter of one hundred. So to multiply by twenty-five: multiply by one hundred and then divide by four — which means halve twice.
screenplay.checkpointPrompt: Why is ×25 the same as ×100 then ÷4?

screenplay.teacherLine: Twenty-five times twelve: twelve times one hundred is twelve hundred. Twelve hundred divided by four — halve to six hundred, halve again to three hundred. Twenty-five times thirty-six: three thousand six hundred divided by four is nine hundred.
Your turn. Twenty-five times four. Multiply by one hundred first.
To divide by five, reverse the multiply-by-five trick: double the number then divide by ten. To divide by twenty-five: multiply by four then divide by one hundred.
screenplay.checkpointPrompt: To divide by 5, what do we do first — multiply or divide?

screenplay.teacherLine: Seventy divided by five: double seventy to one-forty, divide by ten — fourteen. One-thirty-five divided by five: double to two-seventy, divide by ten — twenty-seven. Two hundred divided by twenty-five: multiply by four to eight hundred, divide by one hundred — eight.
Your turn. Divide seventy by five. Double it first.
